user_summary: A research engineer in mechanistic interpretability whose core focus is sparse autoencoders (SAEs) as a practical tool for decomposing superposition in transformer activations. Their three seed papers — Towards Monosemanticity, Scaling Monosemanticity, and Cunningham et al. 2023 — place them squarely in the Anthropic-empirical tradition of dictionary learning for interpretability. They prioritize concrete, empirical results on real models: feature splitting behavior across SAE scales, alternative training objectives (Gated, TopK, JumpReLU, end-to-end KL+MSE) to replace L1, rigorous multi-metric evaluation of learned features (SAEBench-style), and downstream use of SAE features for circuit-level attribution. Their open question — feature transfer across model scales and fine-tunes — sits at a current empirical frontier addressed by only a handful of 2025–2026 papers. They explicitly don't want pure theory, RLHF methodology, capabilities-only work, or policy content.
current_question: How well do SAE features transfer across model scales and fine-tunes? Are there better SAE training objectives than L1?
interests: sparse autoencoder feature splitting at scale
SAE training objectives beyond L1 sparsity
gated TopK JumpReLU sparse autoencoder architectures
dictionary learning for language model interpretability
SAE evaluation benchmarks and proxy metrics
feature geometry in transformer residual stream
SAE feature transfer across model scales
SAE features for fine-tuned model analysis
sparse feature circuits for mechanistic explanation
scaling dictionary learning to frontier models

authors.name: Chris Olah
authors.why: Pioneer of transformer circuits and mechanistic interpretability; primary architect of the research tradition these seed papers belong to.

authors.name: Neel Nanda
authors.why: Leads DeepMind mech interp team; SAEBench co-author; most prolific empirical SAE researcher with broad output on features, circuits, and evaluation.

authors.name: Trenton Bricken
authors.why: Lead author on Towards Monosemanticity; foundational empirical SAE work showing dictionary learning recovers monosemantic features.

authors.name: Adly Templeton
authors.why: Lead author on Scaling Monosemanticity; key empirical work extracting millions of features from Claude 3 Sonnet at production scale.

authors.name: Adam Jermyn
authors.why: Core Anthropic circuits team; co-authored Scaling Monosemanticity and attention-layer mechanistic work.

authors.name: Hoagy Cunningham
authors.why: Lead author on the foundational SAE-interpretability paper (arXiv:2309.08600); early empirical proof that SAEs recover interpretable features.

authors.name: Lee Sharkey
authors.why: Co-authored foundational SAE paper; ongoing work on SAE geometry, canonical units critique, and training objectives at Goodfire.

authors.name: Joshua Batson
authors.why: Co-authored Towards Monosemanticity; interpretability team lead; featured in multiple transformer-circuits updates through 2025.

authors.name: Callum McDougall
authors.why: Co-authored the 2025 circuit tracing paper using attribution graphs; key figure bridging SAE features to circuit-level explanations.

authors.name: Adam Karvonen
authors.why: Lead author of SAEBench; most comprehensive empirical evaluation of SAE training objectives, architectures, and proxy metric reliability.

authors.name: Sam Marks
authors.why: SAEBench co-author; empirical work on SAE feature quality, evaluation methodology, and downstream task performance.

authors.name: Nora Belrose
authors.why: Key research on SAE feature inconsistency across training runs and open-source automated interpretability for SAE features.

authors.name: Dan Braun
authors.why: End-to-end SAE training and attribution-based parameter decomposition; directly relevant to L1 alternatives and functional feature identification.

authors.name: Stefan Heimersheim
authors.why: Empirical work on identifying functionally important SAE features using e2e training; alternative dictionary learning objectives.

authors.name: Tom Lieberum
authors.why: SAE research at scale on Gemma models; circuit tracing work from DeepMind mech interp team; relevant to feature transfer questions.

sources.name: Transformer Circuits Thread
sources.why: Anthropic's primary publication venue for mechanistic interpretability; verified active through May 2026 with circuit tracing, attention, and SAE-adjacent posts.

sources.name: Goodfire Research
sources.why: SAE-focused interpretability lab with papers published through May 2026 on feature geometry, manifold steering, and parameter-space interpretation.

sources.name: Apollo Research Publications
sources.why: Key source for end-to-end SAE training, functionally important feature identification, and mechanistic description length work; verified active March 2026.

sources.name: EleutherAI Blog
sources.why: Active through April 2026; posts on SAE feature consistency across training runs, automated interpretability, and alternative sparse coding architectures.

sources.name: Neuronpedia
sources.why: Open interpretability platform hosting 50M+ SAE latents, SAEBench comparisons, and SAE releases from Anthropic, DeepMind, and OpenAI — essential empirical workspace.

sources.name: DeepMind Safety Research Blog
sources.why: DeepMind mech interp team updates including SAE negative results and the pivot to pragmatic interpretability — important critical counterpoint on SAE limitations.

sources.name: MATS Research
sources.why: Outputs from MATS scholars; origin of SAEBench and several SAE evaluation papers from the independent mech interp community.

sources.name: Neel Nanda's Website
sources.why: Research index and explainers from the most prolific active SAE researcher; canonical reference and pointer to his team's ongoing work.

filter_outs: Pure theory papers with no empirical evaluation
capabilities work that doesn't bear on interpretation
notes: The three seed papers form a tight cluster — all empirical SAE-for-interpretability work in the Anthropic tradition, spanning a one-layer proof-of-concept (Cunningham et al.), the canonical large-scale SAE paper (Towards Monosemanticity), and the frontier scale-up to Claude 3 Sonnet (Scaling Monosemanticity) — confirming a dictionary-learning-first, empiricism-first style. The current question about feature transfer and L1 alternatives is well-supported by active 2025–2026 literature (SAEBench, JumpReLU, e2e SAE, MetaSAEs, the canonical-units critique), so the interests list was tuned to retrieve both the mainstream and the critical/evaluative work. Sources were chosen to cover four active poles of this community: Anthropic-empirical (transformer-circuits.pub), commercial interpretability labs (Goodfire), mechanistic-safety labs (Apollo), and open-source community (EleutherAI blog, Neuronpedia, MATS); all URLs were fetched and confirmed active in 2025–2026.
